meta.title: 6.4 Your First Hook
meta.description: Build a working hook that solves a real annoyance, configure it, and test it in a live session.

blocks.prompt: You are about to configure a hook in your Claude Code settings. Where do you think hook configuration lives — in a separate file, in CLAUDE.md, or somewhere else? How do you think you test whether a hook is working?

blocks.title: Set up the 'protect sensitive files' hook (Hook 1) in your project's .claude/settings.json. Then ask Claude Code to create or edit a file with 'secrets' in the name. Verify that the hook blocks the action. Then ask Claude to edit a normal file and verify the hook allows it through.
blocks.solution: If the hook blocks the sensitive file edit, you should see Claude report that it was prevented. If it also blocks normal files, your regex is too broad — check the grep pattern. If it does not block anything, verify: Is the settings file in the right location? Is the JSON valid? Is the hook under 'PreToolUse'? The most common issue is JSON syntax errors from nested quoting. Try validating your settings.json with a JSON linter.

blocks.prompt: Explain the difference between a PreToolUse hook and a PostToolUse hook to someone who has never heard of hooks. When would you use each one, and what is the critical difference in what they can do?

questions: Did your hook work on the first try? If not, what went wrong and how did you debug it?
Now that you have a working hook, what is the next automatic reaction you want to set up?
How does having automatic safety nets change your approach to giving Claude permission to do things?

blocks.content: Hooks live in .claude/settings.json under a hooks object keyed by event type. Start with a simple hook, test it with echo first, and watch for quoting issues. The most valuable first hook is a PreToolUse safety net that protects files you never want Claude to touch.

blocks.prompt: You have built custom commands (skills) and automatic reactions (hooks). Next, we explore custom subagents — specialized AI workers with specific roles and restrictions. Think of them as employees you hire for particular jobs.
